summary:
The Herero and Nama genocide, carried out by German colonial forces in German South West Africa (present-day Namibia) between 1904 and 1908, is recognized as the first genocide of the twentieth century. The Herero people rose in revolt against German colonial rule in January 1904, following years of land dispossession, cattle confiscation, and brutal treatment. German General Lothar von Trotha responded by issuing an extermination order declaring that Herero people found within German territory would be shot, without exception.

Herero survivors of the Battle of Waterberg were driven into the Omaheke Desert, where German forces guarded the waterholes and allowed those who had fled to die of thirst. Survivors who returned were placed in concentration camps where forced labor, starvation, and disease caused further mass death. Of an estimated 80,000 Herero people before the uprising, around 60,000 to 70,000 perished. The Nama people, who also revolted, suffered proportionally similar losses. Germany formally acknowledged and apologized for the genocide in 2021, though negotiations over reparations continued.
